problem_summary: 2018 MCM Problem B (discrete). Part I: predict the rank of the world's top languages by native (L1) and total speakers over the next 50 years, and forecast their geographic redistribution across continents given global population growth and migration. Part II: advise a multinational service company on where to locate up to six new international offices and whether opening fewer than six is justified. This O-award paper (Team 79002) attacks it as a discrete-time dynamical system: it builds year-by-year difference equations for L1 and L2 speakers of the top 16 languages, a second continent-level difference equation driven by 7 assumed migration routes, and a simple cost-benefit threshold rule that converts a single "cost-profitability" ratio into the optimal office count.
models_used: Model I — discrete-time difference-equation system (1-year step) for L1 and L2 speakers of the top 16 languages, iterated 50 steps in MATLAB
Malthusian (constant-rate exponential) growth for L1 speakers of Type-I languages spoken mainly in developed countries: x(t+1)-x(t)=r·x(t)+R·y(t)
Logistic growth for L1 speakers of Type-II languages spoken mainly in developing countries, with carrying capacity s_i fixed at 2·x_i(0)

Additive L2-speaker increment model: Δy = k1·Σ LangT_ij·x_j + k2·Σ Cult_ij·x_i·x_j + k3·Eco_i + k4·c + k5·Net_i, summing five weighted driver terms (school teaching, cultural/neighborhood+policy contact, GDP/economic power, translation-tech (negative), internet/social-media share)

Model II — continent-level difference equation X^i_j(t+1) for native speakers per language per continent, coupling natural increase rate r^i_j with 7 fixed migration routes and a migration-to-L1 conversion coefficient k6 (implemented in C++)
Gaussian-function curve fit of 1960–2016 World Bank world-population data (chosen over exponential/polynomial/logistic fits): N(t)=1.058e11·exp(((t-2069)/97.67)^2)
Cost-benefit / threshold-optimization model: Profit(city)=α·pop(city)−C, with decision rule open office iff C/α ≤ pop(city), yielding a piecewise table mapping the c-p ratio to 2–6 offices
Local one-at-a-time sensitivity analysis computing an elasticity (%-change-out / %-change-in) for the natural growth rate and each k1..k6
key_techniques: Framing the prediction as a difference-equation (recurrence) system rather than regression, and explicitly justifying this because available census data are non-continuous in time and use inconsistent L2 definitions — turning a data weakness into a modeling-choice rationale
Splitting the 16 languages into developed (Type I, Malthusian) vs developing (Type II, logistic) groups so each gets a demographically appropriate growth law

Decomposing L2 growth into five separately-justified, separately-parameterized driver terms, each tied to a real data source (US foreign-language enrollment pie, GDP-by-language, W3Techs internet-share, social-media language counts)
Encoding qualitative geography as structure: a 'neighborhood effect' adjacency graph between languages and a 'policy-led effect' (Belt-and-Road example) feeding the Cult_ij cultural-exchange matrix

Reducing intractable global migration to 7 named routes with assumed percentage shares, and using indicator functions I{destination==j}, I{language==i} to route immigrants into the right continent/language bucket
Trying multiple fits and reporting the best (Gaussian) for world population, showing comparative model selection

Choosing a continuous proxy output (number of Mandarin L2 speakers) for sensitivity analysis after explicitly arguing that the discrete rank output would be trivially insensitive — a sharp methodological insight

Converting the office-count question into a single interpretable decision parameter (c-p ratio = C/α) and delivering a lookup table, so the client can self-serve the answer once they supply their own cost/profitability
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective it wins on completeness, defensibility, and client-facing usability rather than mathematical sophistication. It answers every sub-question (L1 rank, total rank, geographic distribution, 6 sites, fewer-than-6 decision) with a concrete deliverable and a named, plausible result (e.g. Mandarin becoming #2 native language in North America; Singapore→Rio swap long-term). Every parameter and assumption carries an explicit, common-sense-anchored justification and a cited data source, so the model never feels arbitrary. The developed/developing growth-law split and the five-driver L2 decomposition show genuine domain reasoning. The standout judge-pleasing move is the cost-profitability-ratio threshold table: instead of guessing the company's unknown economics, it parameterizes them and hands back a clean decision rule — exactly the consultant mindset Part II rewards. A full one-at-a-time sensitivity sweep over all six coefficients, the candid weakness list, and a clean executive memo round out a paper that reads as trustworthy and finished."
section_highlights: "Strong structural choices: (1) A tight summary sheet that states the actual before/after top-10 rankings up front, so a judge knows the answers in 30 seconds. (2) A dedicated Assumptions section where each assumption is immediately followed by a 'Reason:' paragraph — assumptions are argued, not just listed. (3) Symbol table and a 16-language index table that make the heavy notation navigable. (4) Logical Part-I build-up: native-speaker growth → L2 growth (five sub-drivers, each its own subsection with its own equation and figure) → combined total difference equations (4) and (5). (5) Results sections pair every model with a concrete table and 3–5 plain-English 'conclusions' explaining *why* (Russia low growth → rank fall; Bangladesh high growth → Bengali rises; Japan negative growth → drops off list). (6) Sensitivity analysis reports a numeric elasticity per parameter and a one-line interpretation each. (7) Honest Strength/Weakness section, executive Memo, and full appendix with raw data tables plus the actual MATLAB and C++ source. The opening rationale for choosing difference equations over regression is a model of turning a constraint into a justified decision."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Heavy reliance on hand-tuned, unestimated coefficients (k1=1/300, k2=1/90000, k3..k5, R, k6) chosen 'by our estimate' — the whole forecast rests on guessed constants, and the appendix code even uses different values (k3=0.3, k4=0.2, k5=0.3) than the main text table (k3=0.2, k4=-0.3, k5=0.2), an internal inconsistency

The sensitivity analysis is partly self-fulfilling: the authors admit rank is discrete and insensitive, then switch to one Mandarin-L2 number and vary each k by only ±a small amount one-at-a-time — it never stresses the model near rank-flip boundaries or varies parameters jointly, so 'robust' is weakly supported
Carrying capacity for logistic languages set arbitrarily to exactly 2·x(0) with no justification, which mechanically caps every developing-country language at double its current L1 base

The Gaussian world-population fit N(t)=1.058e11·exp(+((t−2069)/97.67)^2) is written with a positive exponent (implying explosive growth, not saturation) — likely a transcription error, and the figure shows ~11 billion plateau that the formula doesn't produce

Migration modeled as just 7 fixed routes with assumed fixed percentage shares held constant for 50 years; no feedback from changing populations, no return migration, no second-language effect on geography (acknowledged weakness)

L2 cultural term k2·Cult_ij·x_i·x_j grows with the product of two populations, which can blow up for large languages and is dimensionally awkward; the Cult and LangT matrices are partly filled with a single repeated row of placeholder numbers in the appendix

All linear/proportional assumptions for L2 drivers admitted to be possibly nonlinear; OCR/data tables in the geographic results are visibly garbled (likely artifacts but they expose that the continent-level outputs are hard to audit)
reusable_patterns: Pattern: 'difference-equation-over-regression' justification — when time-series data is sparse, inconsistent, or non-continuous, model dynamics as a recurrence needing only an initial state, and explicitly argue why this beats fitting. Reusable for any forecasting problem with bad longitudinal data.

Pattern: segment the population by regime and apply the right growth law per segment (developed→Malthusian/exponential, developing→logistic with a carrying cap). Generalizes to any heterogeneous-entity growth forecast.

Pattern: additive multi-driver increment — decompose a hard-to-model change into a weighted sum of independently-sourced, independently-parameterized terms (Δ = Σ k_n · factor_n), each with its own data figure. Makes a fuzzy phenomenon defensible.

Pattern: encode qualitative/spatial knowledge as a matrix or adjacency graph (neighborhood/cultural-exchange matrix, indicator-function migration routing) so geography enters a quantitative model cleanly.
Pattern: collapse an under-specified client decision into ONE interpretable threshold parameter (here C/α) and deliver a piecewise lookup table — robust consulting answer when key inputs are unknown.

Pattern: when the headline output is discrete/insensitive (a rank), pick a continuous internal quantity as the sensitivity target and state why. Avoids a vacuous 'rank never changes' sensitivity section.
Pattern: try several fits, show the comparison, keep the best (model selection as a visible step rather than a single asserted fit).

Pattern: results presentation discipline — every model gets {result table + 3-5 'why' bullets + sensitivity number}; pair the memo/executive summary with a data+code appendix to signal reproducibility.
